air Political Practices Commissioll 
P.o. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUilDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Technical Assistance/ Administration 
(916) 322·5660 
Executive/legal 
322·5901 
August 2, 1976 
John F. Van DeKamp 
District Attorney 
County of Los Angeles 
Office of the District Attorney 
18000 Criminal Courts Building 
210 West Temple Street 
Los Angeles 
CA 
90012 
Dear Mr. Van DeKamp: 
Enforcement/Conflict of Interest 
322·6441 
Thank you for your letter of July 20, 1976, inquiring 
about the applicability of the opinion requested by the 
Honorable Kenneth Cory, 1 FPPC Ops. 137 (No. 75-094-C, Oct. 1, 
1975) to the situation where an elected officer attends, at no 
expense, fundraising dinners or testimonials conducted for 
charitable purposes. 
It is the view of the staff that free 
sion and incidental benefits which are provided in 
connection with an elected officer's attendance at the types 
functions to which you refer will not create any reporting 
obligations under the Political Reform Act. 
Attendance at such functions by an elected officer involves 
what is essentially a political activity and, therefore, would be 
reportable, if at all, under Chapter 4 of the Political Reform 
Act. 
In light of the fact that Chapter 4 contains the relevant 
reporting criteria, the reasoning employed in the opinion requested 
by Mr. Cory is applicable to the types of functions you describe 
and the attendance of the elected officer constitutes adequate 
consideration for the benefits he receives. 
If you have any further questions, please do not hesitate 
to contact me. 
Kenneth Finne 
Chief 
1 Division 


COUNTY OF LOS ANGELES 
OFFICE OF THE DISTRICT ATTORNEY 
18000 CRIMINAL COURTS BUILDING 
210 WEST TEMPLE STREET 
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90012 
974-3501 
,JOHN K. VAN DE KAMp, DISTRICT ATTORNEY 
July 20, 1976 
¥rr. Kenneth Finney, Director 
Legal Division 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
1100 K Street 
Sacramento, California 95814 
Dear Mr. Finney: 
I have received and read with interest the Commission's 
Opinion No. 75-094-C wherein the Commission responds to an 
inquiry by The Honorable Kenneth Cory. 
The conclusion of 
the Commission was as follows: 
"If an elected state officer attends a political 
fund raising dinner at the invitation of the sponsors 
without purchasing a ticket, the officer has no report-
ing obligations under the Political Reform Act. 
This 
conclusion is not altered by the fact that the officer 
attends the dinner for the specified purpose of making 
a speech." 
An elected officer also receives, with some frequency, 
invitations from sponsors to attend, at no expense, fund 
raising dinners or testimonials conducted for charitable 
purposes. 
I would assume that the same reasoning applies to this 
situation as to the previous question posed by Mr. Cory. 
Again, by virtue of his attendance, a benefit constituting 
adequate consideration is conferred upon the sponsors by 
the invited officer. 
Hence, no reporting obligation under 
the Political Reform Act arises. 
I write to inquire if 
this assumption is correct. 
Sincerely, 
mw 
VAl'J DE KAlylP 
Attorney 
cc: 
chard 
ller, Esq. 
iance and Enforcement Section

sections.question_synthetic: Whether an elected officer's attendance at no-expense fundraising dinners or testimonials for charitable purposes creates reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act, and whether the reasoning from FPPC Opinion No. 75-094-C (regarding political fundraising dinners) applies.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Free admission and incidental benefits provided at charitable fundraising dinners do not create reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act. The reasoning from the Cory opinion applies, as the elected officer's attendance constitutes adequate consideration for the benefits received.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document includes both incoming request from DA Van DeKamp (July 20, 1976) and FPPC response (August 2, 1976). Clear question-and-answer structure. High OCR quality.

embedding.summary: FPPC responds to District Attorney Van DeKamp's inquiry about whether an elected officer's attendance at charitable fundraising dinners and testimonials (at no expense) creates reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act. The FPPC concludes that such attendance does not create reporting obligations, applying the reasoning from the prior Cory opinion.
